paper_metadata.title: Emotions and Policy Views
paper_metadata.authors: Yann Algan; Eva Davoine; Thomas Renault; Stefanie Stantcheva
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Two online survey experiments with nationally representative U.S. adults; climate-policy Survey B fielded Nov–Dec 2024 with roughly 5,000–6,000 respondents after quality screens.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Survey B describes a hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers that raises gasoline prices by $0.40/gal and rebates revenues as $600/year per adult, plus a progressive carbon tax on high-income households, and studies how emotional treatments and covariates affect support.

determinants.definition: Respondents view a wildfire video emphasizing negligence and corporate/government blame, designed to elicit anger about climate change.
determinants.mechanism: Anger heightens perceived injustice and responsibility for climate damage, mobilizing support for costly mitigation tools like a carbon tax with rebates.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. federal carbon tax with cash transfers that raises gasoline price by $0.40 per gallon and returns revenues as $600 per adult per year.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: “Anger leads to stronger beliefs in human-caused climate change, increased support for climate policies (e.g., carbon taxes or subsidies for electric vehicles)… Anger treatment has a significant positive effect across all climate indices.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Anger treatment coefficient on carbon-tax support ≈ 0.09 (p<0.10, standardized); ≈ 0.72 (p<0.01) on broader climate policy views index.
determinants.notes: Effect estimated in OLS with controls for gender, age, income, education, and 2024 vote.

determinants.definition: Respondents view climate-disaster videos highlighting terrifying wildfires without attributing blame, designed to elicit fear.
determinants.mechanism: Fear increases perceived danger but, in this design, does not translate into higher willingness to back a carbon tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: “By contrast, the fear treatment has no significant effect on any of these outcomes.”
determinants.page_or_section: Section 5.3; Appendix Tables A18 and A20, col.4
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Fear treatment coefficient on carbon-tax support ≈ 0.07, not statistically significant; no significant effect on climate indices.
determinants.notes: Null effect despite strong increase in self-reported fear, suggesting fear is less policy‑mobilizing than anger.

determinants.definition: Respondents view serene landscape videos with peaceful music, designed to elicit joy and tranquility.
determinants.mechanism: Relaxation and positive affect may reduce perceived urgency or threat, slightly lowering willingness to endorse a costly carbon tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: In Table A20, the positive emotion treatment has a small negative coefficient on support for the carbon tax with cash transfers.
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Positive-emotion treatment coefficient ≈ −0.07 (p<0.10) on standardized carbon-tax support.
determinants.notes: Authors stress that positive emotions do not systematically offset negative ones; this effect is only marginally significant.

determinants.definition: Indicator for respondents who voted (or would have voted) for Donald Trump in the 2024 presidential election.
determinants.mechanism: Reflects conservative and climate-skeptical ideology, strongly associated with opposition to carbon taxation even after controlling for demographics.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: In the carbon tax support regression, the Trump coefficient is −0.52*** (Table A20, col.4).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Trump voter coefficient ≈ −0.52 (p<0.01), about half a standard deviation lower support than Harris voters, conditional on controls.
determinants.notes: Represents partisan divide in carbon-tax acceptability within the U.S. sample.

determinants.definition: Indicator equal to one for female respondents; male is the reference category.
determinants.mechanism: Women may be more sensitive to household cost increases or skeptical of the specific tax design, resulting in lower stated support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: Female coefficient is −0.10*** in the carbon-tax support regression (Table A20, col.4).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Female coefficient ≈ −0.10 (p<0.01) on standardized carbon-tax support, conditional on other covariates.
determinants.notes: Despite often higher environmental concern, women express lower support for this specific carbon tax design.

determinants.definition: Dummy for respondents aged 50–69; 18–29-year-olds are the reference group in regressions.
determinants.mechanism: Older adults may be more resistant to new taxes or more focused on short‑term cost burdens, reducing support for a carbon tax with rebates.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical U.S. carbon tax with cash transfers raising gasoline price by $0.40/gal and rebating $600/year per adult.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for carbon tax with cash transfers (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: Age 50–69 coefficient is −0.14*** in the carbon-tax support regression (Table A20, col.4).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Age 50–69 coefficient ≈ −0.14 (p<0.01) on standardized carbon-tax support.
determinants.notes: Age 30–49 shows near‑zero effect; opposition is concentrated among older adults.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Anger-inducing climate video (vs no video) – progressive carbon tax
determinants.definition: Same anger video as above; here effect measured on support for a progressive carbon tax on high-income people.
determinants.mechanism: Anger about culpable actors increases demand for punitive, redistributive climate instruments like a progressive carbon tax on the rich.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax that would raise gasoline prices by $0.40 per gallon for high-income people only.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: Anger treatment has a significant positive effect on support for a progressive carbon tax and on redistributive climate policies (Appendix Table A21).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Anger treatment coefficient on progressive carbon-tax support ≈ 0.14 (p<0.01).
determinants.notes: Although authors highlight funding for vulnerable groups, support for the progressive carbon tax also rises significantly.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Fear-inducing climate video (vs no video) – progressive carbon tax
determinants.definition: Fear-focused wildfire videos without blame; effect measured on support for progressive carbon tax.
determinants.mechanism: Despite increasing fear, the treatment does not significantly alter support for taxing high-income households’ gasoline use.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax that raises gasoline price by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: “By contrast, the fear treatment has no significant effect on any of these outcomes.”
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Fear treatment coefficient on progressive carbon-tax support ≈ 0.02, not statistically significant.
determinants.notes: Reinforces that not all negative emotions are equally effective at shifting carbon-tax views.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Trump voter (vs Harris voter) – progressive carbon tax
determinants.definition: Indicator for respondents who voted (or would have voted) for Donald Trump in 2024, relative to Harris voters.
determinants.mechanism: Reflects ideological opposition to redistributive and climate policies, yielding strong resistance to a progressive carbon tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax raising gasoline prices by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: Trump coefficient is −0.62*** for support of the progressive carbon tax (Table A21, col.1).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Trump voter coefficient ≈ −0.62 (p<0.01) on standardized progressive carbon-tax support.
determinants.notes: Partisan gap for progressive carbon tax is even larger than for the uniform carbon tax with cash transfers.

determinants.definition: Female dummy; male is reference group in regressions for progressive carbon-tax support.
determinants.mechanism: Women show slightly lower support for taxing gasoline only for high-income households, possibly due to concerns about policy complexity or skepticism toward targeted taxes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax raising gasoline prices by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: Female coefficient is −0.07* for progressive carbon-tax support (Table A21, col.1).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Female coefficient ≈ −0.07 (p<0.10) on standardized progressive carbon-tax support.

determinants.definition: Dummy for respondents aged 30–49; 18–29-year-olds are the comparison group.
determinants.mechanism: Middle‑aged adults may anticipate bearing some incidence of higher gasoline prices despite being above student/low‑income status, reducing support for a rich‑only carbon tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax raising gasoline prices by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: Age 30–49 coefficient is −0.12** in the progressive carbon-tax support regression (Table A21, col.1).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Age 30–49 coefficient ≈ −0.12 (p<0.05) on standardized progressive carbon-tax support.

determinants.definition: Indicator for respondents aged 50–69 compared to 18–29-year-olds.
determinants.mechanism: Older respondents are substantially less supportive of redistributive carbon taxation, likely reflecting tax aversion and lower perceived personal benefits from long-run climate mitigation.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive carbon tax raising gasoline prices by $0.40/gal for high-income only.
determinants.outcome_variable: Standardized support for progressive carbon tax on high-income households (5-point Likert).
determinants.evidence_quote: Age 50–69 coefficient is −0.34*** for progressive carbon-tax support (Table A21, col.1).
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Age 50–69 coefficient ≈ −0.34 (p<0.01) on standardized progressive carbon-tax support.
determinants.notes: Indicates a strong age gradient in favourability toward taxing high‑income emitters.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Climate policy indices include non-tax items
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Several treatment effects are on composite indices mixing support for carbon taxes with other climate policies (e.g., EV subsidies, redistributive funding). Extracted determinants rely on columns where the dependent variable is specifically a carbon-tax item.
